examples.title: Simple Turing Test Simulator
examples.description: Basic implementation of a Turing Test scenario where evaluator asks questions
examples.code:
PROCEDURE TuringTest()
 humanResponses ← []
 machineResponses ← []
 questions ← ["What is 2+2?", "Tell me a joke", "What is love?"]
 
 FOR each question IN questions DO
 PRINT "Question to Participant: " + question
 hResponse ← INPUT "Enter human response:"
 mResponse ← INPUT "Enter machine response:"
 APPEND hResponse TO humanResponses
 APPEND mResponse TO machineResponses
 END FOR
 
 evaluatorGuess ← INPUT "Which responses are human? (1 or 2):"
 
 IF evaluatorGuess == "Correct" THEN
 PRINT "Machine passes Turing Test!"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Machine fails Turing Test."
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates a simple Turing Test setup where an evaluator asks questions to both a human and a machine, then tries to identify which responses came from which participant.

examples.title: Simple Rule-Based (Symbolic) AI System
examples.description: Expert System shell demonstrating symbolic AI approach
examples.code:
RULEBASE MedicalDiagnosis
 RULES ← [
 {condition: "fever AND cough", action: "Flu"},
 {condition: "headache AND fatigue", action: "Migraine"},
 {condition: "chest_pain AND breathing_difficulty", action: "Heart Problem"}
 ]
 
 PROCEDURE Diagnose(symptoms)
 FOR each rule IN RULES DO
 IF Match(rule.condition, symptoms) THEN
 RETURN rule.action
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN "Unknown condition"
 END PROCEDURE
 
 INPUT patientSymptoms ← {fever: TRUE, cough: TRUE}
 diagnosis ← Diagnose(patientSymptoms)
 PRINT "Diagnosis: " + diagnosis
END RULEBASE
examples.explanation: This demonstrates Symbolic AI (GOFAI) using explicit IF-THEN rules to make decisions. The system uses a knowledge base of rules rather than learning from data.

examples.title: Simple Machine Learning Classifier
examples.description: Basic ML concept showing learning from data
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SimpleMLClassifier()
 // Training Data
 trainingData ← [
 {features: [1, 0], label: "Cat"},
 {features: [0, 1], label: "Dog"},
 {features: [1, 1], label: "Cat"},
 {features: [0, 0], label: "Dog"}
 ]
 
 // Simple Learning Algorithm
 weights ← [0.5, 0.5]
 learningRate ← 0.1
 epochs ← 100
 
 FOR i FROM 1 TO epochs DO
 FOR each sample IN trainingData DO
 prediction ← DotProduct(sample.features, weights)
 error ← sample.label - prediction
 weights ← weights + (learningRate × error × sample.features)
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 // Prediction
 testSample ← [1, 0]
 result ← DotProduct(testSample, weights)
 PRINT "Predicted Label: " + (IF result > 0.5 THEN "Cat" ELSE "Dog")
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the core ML concept: learning weights from training data. Unlike symbolic AI, the system learns patterns from examples rather than using explicit rules.
